schools.summary: The call up the mount to worship afar off: 'and he said unto Moses, Come up unto the LORD, thou, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel; and worship ye afar off' — God calling Moses, with Aaron, his two sons Nadab and Abihu, and seventy elders, to come up the mount (the representatives, the chosen company, the heads of Israel, summoned to draw near, the honor, the elders called to a special nearness, the covenant about to be ratified, vv.9-11); the call to come up (to draw near to God, the representatives admitted) — the approach to God (the privilege of the chosen); 'worship ye afar off' (the worship at a distance, the elders to worship from afar, not coming as near as Moses, v.2, the reverence, the distance kept, the gradations of nearness, Moses alone admitted to the closest, vv.2, the providence, the reverent approach, the order of the covenant, a faint figure of the access opened in Christ, who brings near them that were afar off, Ephesians 2:13) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And he said unto Moses, Come up unto the LORD, thou, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel; and worship ye afar off
the call up the mount to worship afar off
the representatives drawn near, yet the distance kept in reverence
a faint figure of the access opened in Christ (Ephesians 2:13)

schools.summary: Moses alone admitted nearest to God: 'and Moses alone shall come near the LORD: but they shall not come nigh; neither shall the people go up with him' — Moses alone to come near to the LORD (to the closest), while the elders worshipped afar off (v.1) and the people did not go up at all (the gradations of nearness, Moses nearest, the elders farther, the people at the foot, the office of Moses, the mediator, admitted to the closest fellowship with God); Moses alone coming near (the mediator, the closest access) — the office (Moses the go-between, the type of Christ, the one Mediator, who alone enters the holiest, Hebrews 9:24, the singular nearness); 'they shall not come nigh; neither shall the people go up with him' (the elders and people kept back, the gradations of access, the reverence, the providence, the distance under the law, the mediator alone admitted, a figure of the access through Christ alone, John 14:6; Hebrews 10:19-20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Moses alone shall come near the LORD: but they shall not come nigh; neither shall the people go up with him
Moses alone admitted nearest to God
the mediator admitted to the closest fellowship (Hebrews 9:24)
a type of Christ who alone enters the holiest (Hebrews 10:19-20)

schools.summary: The people's unanimous promise to keep the covenant: 'and Moses came and told the people all the words of the LORD, and all the judgments: and all the people answered with one voice, and said, All the words which the LORD hath said will we do' — Moses rehearsing to the people all God's words and judgments (the covenant terms, the moral and judicial law, chs. 20-23), and the people, with one voice, promising to do them all (the covenant proposed, the law set before the people, the unanimous consent, the whole people, with one voice, engaging to obey); Moses telling all the words and judgments (the full covenant, faithfully) — the rehearsal (the covenant set before them); 'all the people answered with one voice... will we do' (the unanimous promise, the people engaging to obey, as Exodus 19:8, the covenant accepted, the people's vow, sincere yet self-confident, soon to fail, ch. 32; Deuteronomy 5:27-29, the providence, the covenant engaged, the law a schoolmaster, Galatians 3:24) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Moses came and told the people all the words of the LORD, and all the judgments: and all the people answered with one voice, and said, All the words which the LORD hath said will we do
the people's unanimous promise to keep the covenant
the people's vow, sincere yet self-confident (Exodus 19:8; Deuteronomy 5:27-29)
the covenant engaged, the law a schoolmaster (Galatians 3:24)

schools.summary: Moses writing the covenant and building the altar and pillars: 'and Moses wrote all the words of the LORD, and rose up early in the morning, and builded an altar under the hill, and twelve pillars, according to the twelve tribes of Israel' — Moses writing down all God's words (the book of the covenant, v.7), and early in the morning building an altar at the foot of the mount, with twelve pillars (the written covenant, the words recorded, the inspired Scripture, the altar, for the sacrifices of the covenant, vv.5-6, the twelve pillars, for the twelve tribes, the parties to the covenant); the altar (for the covenant sacrifices, representing God, one party to the covenant) — the place of sacrifice (the meeting of God and the people in the blood); 'twelve pillars, according to the twelve tribes of Israel' (the twelve pillars, representing the twelve tribes, the people, the other party to the covenant, the covenant between God, the altar, and the people, the pillars, the providence, the solemn ratification, the parties represented) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Moses wrote all the words of the LORD... and builded an altar under the hill, and twelve pillars, according to the twelve tribes of Israel
Moses writing the covenant and building the altar and pillars
the written covenant, the inspired Scripture recorded
the covenant between God (the altar) and the people (the twelve pillars)

schools.summary: The burnt offerings and peace offerings of the covenant: 'and he sent young men of the children of Israel, which offered burnt offerings, and sacrificed peace offerings of oxen unto the LORD' — Moses sending young men (the firstborn, or chosen youths, before the Aaronic priesthood was established) to offer burnt offerings and peace offerings of oxen (the sacrifices of the covenant, the burnt offering for atonement and dedication, the peace offering for communion and fellowship, the blood, provided for the sprinkling, vv.6-8); the young men (the firstborn, or chosen, acting as priests before Aaron's consecration) — the offerers (the sacrifices presented); 'burnt offerings, and sacrificed peace offerings of oxen' (the burnt offering, wholly consumed, for atonement and dedication, Leviticus 1, and the peace offering, for communion, the covenant fellowship, Leviticus 3, the covenant sealed in blood and fellowship, the providence, the sacrifices the ground of the covenant, the blood for the sprinkling, vv.6-8, a type of Christ, the burnt offering and peace offering, the atonement and the peace, Ephesians 2:14-16) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And he sent young men of the children of Israel, which offered burnt offerings, and sacrificed peace offerings of oxen unto the LORD
the burnt offerings and peace offerings of the covenant
the atonement (burnt offering) and the communion (peace offering)
a type of Christ the offering, the atonement and the peace (Ephesians 2:14-16)

schools.summary: The blood divided between the altar and the people: 'and Moses took half of the blood, and put it in basons; and half of the blood he sprinkled on the altar' — Moses dividing the blood of the sacrifices into two halves: half put in basins (for the people, vv.8), half sprinkled on the altar (for God, the dividing of the blood, the two parties to the covenant, God, represented by the altar, and the people, both sharing in the one blood, the covenant in blood, the blood the bond and seal of the covenant, vv.8); the half in basins (reserved for sprinkling on the people, vv.8) — the people's share (the blood for them); 'half of the blood he sprinkled on the altar' (the half on the altar, for God, the other party, the covenant in blood, the blood applied to both parties, God and the people, the bond of the covenant, the providence, the blood of the covenant, vv.8, a type of the blood of Christ, the blood of the new covenant, Matthew 26:28; Hebrews 9:18-22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Moses took half of the blood, and put it in basons; and half of the blood he sprinkled on the altar
the blood divided between the altar and the people
the two parties to the covenant - God (the altar) and the people, sharing the one blood
a type of the blood of Christ, the blood of the new covenant (Matthew 26:28; Hebrews 9:18-22)

schools.summary: The reading of the book of the covenant and the people's renewed promise: 'and he took the book of the covenant, and read in the audience of the people: and they said, All that the LORD hath said will we do, and be obedient' — Moses reading the book of the covenant (the words he had written, v.4) aloud to the people, and they renewing their promise: 'All that the LORD hath said will we do, and be obedient' (the public reading, the covenant terms read aloud, the people hearing, the renewed consent, the people promising obedience, now adding 'and be obedient,' the fuller engagement); the 'book of the covenant' (the written law, the inspired Scripture, the covenant document, cited Hebrews 9:19) — the written covenant (the basis of the engagement); 'will we do, and be obedient' (the renewed, fuller promise, the people engaging both to do and to obey, the full submission, the covenant accepted, yet, the witnesses note, in self-confidence, soon broken, ch. 32; Deuteronomy 5:29, the providence, the covenant ratified, the people's vow, the blood to seal it, v.8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, JFB.

themes: And he took the book of the covenant, and read in the audience of the people: and they said, All that the LORD hath said will we do, and be obedient
the reading of the book of the covenant and the people's renewed promise
the written covenant read aloud, cited in Hebrews 9:19
the people's fuller vow, soon broken in self-confidence (Deuteronomy 5:29)

schools.summary: The blood of the covenant sprinkled on the people: 'and Moses took the blood, and sprinkled it on the people, and said, Behold the blood of the covenant, which the LORD hath made with you concerning all these words' — Moses sprinkling the reserved half of the blood (v.6) on the people, declaring it 'the blood of the covenant' (the sprinkling, the blood applied to the people, sealing them into the covenant, the covenant ratified in blood, the bond sealed, the people bound and consecrated by the blood); the type — 'Behold the blood of the covenant': the blood that sealed the old covenant a type of the blood of Christ, the blood of the new covenant (the witnesses noting the New Testament fulfillment — the Lord, instituting the supper, taking the cup and saying, 'This is my blood of the new testament (covenant), which is shed for many for the remission of sins,' Matthew 26:28; Mark 14:24; Luke 22:20, 'the new testament in my blood,' echoing this very word, and the Epistle to the Hebrews expounding it at length — 'for where a testament is, there must also of necessity be the death of the testator... whereupon neither the first testament was dedicated without blood. For when Moses had spoken every precept to all the people... he took the blood... and sprinkled both the book, and all the people, saying, This is the blood of the testament which God hath enjoined unto you,' Hebrews 9:16-22, 'without shedding of blood is no remission,' the covenant sealed in blood, no covenant, no remission, without blood, the blood of Christ the blood of the everlasting covenant, Hebrews 13:20; 1 Peter 1:2, 'the sprinkling of the blood of Jesus Christ'; 10:29); 'concerning all these words' (the covenant of all the words, the law, the terms, sealed by the blood, the binding, the people bound to the whole covenant by the blood, the providence, the type of the new covenant in Christ's blood, the better covenant, Hebrews 8:6; 12:24) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: What does 'the blood of the covenant' signify?

sides.position: The blood sealing the old covenant, a type of the blood of Christ the new covenant (Matthew 26:28; Hebrews 9:16-22) - the covenant sealed in blood ('without shedding of blood is no remission'), the blood of Christ the blood of the everlasting covenant (Hebrews 13:20; 1 Peter 1:2)

themes: And Moses took the blood, and sprinkled it on the people, and said, Behold the blood of the covenant, which the LORD hath made with you concerning all these words
the blood of the covenant sprinkled on the people (the crux of the covenant blood)
a type of the blood of Christ, the blood of the new covenant (Matthew 26:28; Hebrews 9:16-22)
the covenant sealed in blood, without which is no remission (Hebrews 13:20; 1 Peter 1:2)

schools.summary: The elders going up to God: 'then went up Moses, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel' — after the covenant was sealed in blood (v.8), Moses, Aaron, his sons, and the seventy elders going up the mount (as called, v.1) to the special nearness (the ascent, the representatives going up to God, the covenant fellowship, the sealed covenant opening the way to the vision and the meal, vv.10-11, the honor, the elders admitted to behold God); the company going up (Moses, Aaron, his sons, the elders, as summoned, v.1) — the representatives (the ascent); 'and seventy of the elders of Israel' (the seventy elders, the representatives of the people, going up to behold God, vv.10-11, the covenant fellowship, the sealed covenant the ground of the nearness, the providence, the vision and the meal following the blood, the access through the covenant) — Henry, Gill.

themes: Then went up Moses, and Aaron, Nadab, and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel
the elders going up to God
the representatives going up after the blood was sprinkled (v.8)
the sealed covenant opening the way to the vision (vv.10-11)

schools.summary: The vision of the God of Israel: 'and they saw the God of Israel: and there was under his feet as it were a paved work of a sapphire stone, and as it were the body of heaven in his clearness' — the wonder — 'they saw the God of Israel': the elders, going up, 'saw the God of Israel' (the witnesses weighing the manner of the vision — not that they saw God in his essence, for 'no man hath seen God at any time,' John 1:18, 'thou canst not see my face: for there shall no man see me, and live,' Exodus 33:20, 'whom no man hath seen, nor can see,' 1 Timothy 6:16, but they saw a visible manifestation, a glorious appearance, a representation of God's presence and majesty, the glory under his feet, vv.10b, perhaps the pre-incarnate Christ, the Word, the visible image of the invisible God, John 1:18, 'the only begotten Son... he hath declared him,' the Angel of the covenant, in whom God revealed himself, the gracious condescension, God showing himself to the elders, in a manifestation they could bear, the covenant privilege, the sealed covenant opening the way to behold God, the foretaste of the beatific vision, the pure in heart shall see God, Matthew 5:8; 1 John 3:2); the pavement under his feet (a sapphire-blue, transparent splendor, like the very body of heaven for clearness) — the glorious manifestation (the pavement of sapphire, the blue of heaven, the throne above the firmament, Ezekiel 1:26, 'the likeness of a throne, as the appearance of a sapphire stone'; Revelation 4:3, the majesty of the appearance); 'as it were the body of heaven in his clearness' (the clear, bright, transparent heaven, the splendor of the manifestation, the glory, the visible token of God's presence and majesty, the providence, the gracious vision granted to the covenant representatives, the foretaste of seeing God) — Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: How did the elders 'see the God of Israel'?

sides.position: Not the divine essence (John 1:18; Exodus 33:20; 1 Timothy 6:16) but a visible manifestation of his glory and majesty (perhaps the pre-incarnate Word who declares the Father) - a gracious condescension and covenant privilege, a foretaste of the beatific vision (Matthew 5:8; 1 John 3:2); the sapphire pavement the token of his throne (Ezekiel 1:26)

themes: And they saw the God of Israel: and there was under his feet as it were a paved work of a sapphire stone, and as it were the body of heaven in his clearness
the vision of the God of Israel (the crux of seeing God)
not the divine essence (John 1:18; Exodus 33:20) but a visible manifestation of glory
a covenant privilege, a foretaste of the beatific vision (Matthew 5:8; 1 John 3:2); the sapphire throne (Ezekiel 1:26)

schools.summary: The covenant meal in the presence of God: 'and upon the nobles of the children of Israel he laid not his hand: also they saw God, and did eat and drink' — God 'laid not his hand' upon the nobles (the elders), that is, did not strike or destroy them, though they saw him (for sinful man could not ordinarily behold God and live, Exodus 33:20; Judges 13:22, the gracious sparing, the elders, beholding God, preserved by his mercy, not consumed, the wonder, that sinful men saw God and lived); 'they saw God' (the manifestation, v.10) and were spared — the gracious vision (the mercy that preserved them); the covenant meal — 'did eat and drink': the elders eating and drinking (of the peace offerings, the covenant sacrifices, v.5) in the presence of God (the witnesses noting the meaning — the covenant feast, the fellowship meal that sealed the covenant, the eating before God a token of reconciliation and communion, the covenant ratified in a shared meal, as covenants were sealed with a feast, Genesis 31:54, the peace offering eaten in fellowship with God, the lesson — the communion of the covenant, the reconciled people feasting in God's presence, a foretaste of the marriage supper, Revelation 19:9, the Lord's table, the communion of the new covenant, 1 Corinthians 10:16-21, the eating and drinking in the kingdom, Luke 22:30, the peace and joy of the covenant fellowship) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'they saw God, and did eat and drink' signify?

sides.position: The elders graciously spared though they beheld God (Exodus 33:20), and the covenant meal - the fellowship feast that sealed the covenant (the peace offerings eaten before God, Genesis 31:54), the communion of the reconciled people, a foretaste of the marriage supper and the Lord's table (Revelation 19:9; 1 Corinthians 10:16-21; Luke 22:30)

themes: And upon the nobles of the children of Israel he laid not his hand: also they saw God, and did eat and drink
the covenant meal in the presence of God (the crux of the fellowship feast)
the elders graciously spared though they beheld God (Exodus 33:20)
the fellowship feast of the covenant, a foretaste of the marriage supper (Genesis 31:54; Revelation 19:9; 1 Corinthians 10:16-21)

schools.summary: God calling Moses up for the tables of the law: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Come up to me into the mount, and be there: and I will give thee tables of stone, and a law, and commandments which I have written; that thou mayest teach them' — God calling Moses higher up the mount, to remain there (the forty days, v.18), to receive the tables of stone with the law and commandments God had written (the call, Moses summoned to the longer, closer communion, the tables, the stone tables of the Ten Commandments, written by God, Exodus 31:18; 32:16); the tables of stone (the Ten Commandments, written by God's own finger, Exodus 31:18, 'written with the finger of God'; 32:16, 'the writing of God, graven upon the tables') — the divine authorship (the law God's own writing, the permanence, graven in stone, the enduring moral law); 'that thou mayest teach them' (the law given to Moses to teach the people, the mediator receiving and teaching the law, the providence, the moral law, written by God, given for the instruction of the people, the abiding rule, Matthew 5:17-19, the law written by God on stone, the new covenant law written on the heart, Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 3:3) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Come up to me into the mount, and be there: and I will give thee tables of stone, and a law, and commandments which I have written; that thou mayest teach them
God calling Moses up for the tables of the law
the moral law written by God's own finger on stone (Exodus 31:18; 32:16)
given for the people's instruction, the law written on the heart in the new covenant (Jeremiah 31:33; 2 Corinthians 3:3)

schools.summary: Moses and Joshua going up the mount of God: 'and Moses rose up, and his minister Joshua: and Moses went up into the mount of God' — Moses rising, with his minister (attendant, servant) Joshua, and going up into the mount of God (the ascent, Moses going up for the forty days, vv.18, Joshua his minister, the faithful attendant, the future leader, accompanying Moses part of the way, the mount of God, Sinai, the place of the law); Joshua, Moses' minister (the faithful servant and attendant, accompanying him) — the minister (the future leader trained in attendance on Moses); 'Moses went up into the mount of God' (Moses going up, to the closer communion, the forty days, vv.18, the mount of God, Sinai, Exodus 3:1, the providence, the mediator going up to receive the law, leaving Joshua, perhaps, on the way, vv.14) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Moses rose up, and his minister Joshua: and Moses went up into the mount of God
Moses and Joshua going up the mount of God
the mediator going up to receive the law (vv.18)
Joshua the faithful minister and future leader attending Moses

schools.summary: The elders charged to govern in Moses' absence: 'and he said unto the elders, Tarry ye here for us, until we come again unto you: and, behold, Aaron and Hur are with you: if any man have any matters to do, let him come unto them' — Moses charging the elders to wait until he returned, leaving Aaron and Hur in charge to handle any matters (disputes, business, the provision for government, the people not left without order in Moses' absence, the deputies, Aaron and Hur appointed to judge and govern, vv.18, the orderly arrangement); the elders charged to wait (until Moses returned) — the order (the people kept in their place); 'Aaron and Hur are with you' (the deputies, Aaron and Hur, to govern and judge in Moses' absence, as in the battle with Amalek, Exodus 17:10-12, the provision, the people's matters handled, the order maintained, the providence, the government provided for, yet the absence the occasion of the golden calf, ch. 32, when the people corrupted themselves) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And he said unto the elders, Tarry ye here for us, until we come again unto you: and, behold, Aaron and Hur are with you: if any man have any matters to do, let him come unto them
the elders charged to govern in Moses' absence
Aaron and Hur appointed deputies to judge and govern (Exodus 17:10-12)
the order maintained, yet the absence the occasion of the golden calf (ch. 32)

schools.summary: Moses entering the cloud on the mount: 'and Moses went up into the mount, and a cloud covered the mount' — Moses going up, and a cloud covering the mount (the cloud, the visible token of God's presence, the glory veiled, Exodus 19:9, 16, the cloud into which Moses entered, vv.18, the presence of God, the cloud covering the mount, the glory descending, the prelude to the closer communion, the cloud the veil of the divine presence); Moses going up (to the closer communion, the forty days) — the ascent (the mediator entering the presence); 'a cloud covered the mount' (the cloud, the token of God's presence, the glory veiled, Exodus 19:16, the covering, the mount wrapped in the cloud, the glory within, vv.16-17, the providence, the cloud the veil of the divine presence, into which Moses would enter, vv.18, a figure of the cloud of glory, the shekinah, that would fill the tabernacle, Exodus 40:34) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And Moses went up into the mount, and a cloud covered the mount
Moses entering the cloud on the mount
the cloud the token of God's presence, the glory veiled (Exodus 19:16)
a figure of the cloud of glory that would fill the tabernacle (Exodus 40:34)

schools.summary: The glory of the LORD abiding on the mount: 'and the glory of the LORD abode upon mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it six days: and the seventh day he called unto Moses out of the midst of the cloud' — the glory of the LORD (the shekinah, the visible splendor of God's presence) abiding on Sinai, the cloud covering it six days, and on the seventh day God calling Moses from the midst of the cloud (the abiding glory, God's presence resting on the mount, the six days' waiting, Moses, perhaps, waiting six days before being called in, the seventh-day call, the sabbath, or the appointed day, God calling Moses into the cloud); the glory of the LORD (the shekinah, the visible manifestation of God's presence, Exodus 40:34, the glory that filled the mount) — the abiding presence (the majesty of God); 'the seventh day he called unto Moses out of the midst of the cloud' (God calling Moses, on the seventh day, into the very midst of the cloud, the closest communion, vv.18, the call into the presence, the mediator admitted into the cloud of glory, the providence, the closer communion, the receiving of the law, vv.18, the waiting and the call) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And the glory of the LORD abode upon mount Sinai, and the cloud covered it six days: and the seventh day he called unto Moses out of the midst of the cloud
the glory of the LORD abiding on the mount
the abiding shekinah, God's presence resting on Sinai (Exodus 40:34)
the mediator called into the midst of the cloud of glory (vv.18)

schools.summary: The glory of the LORD like devouring fire: 'and the sight of the glory of the LORD was like devouring fire on the top of the mount in the eyes of the children of Israel' — the appearance — 'the sight of the glory of the LORD was like devouring fire on the top of the mount in the eyes of the children of Israel': to the people below, the glory of the LORD on the top of the mount appearing like a devouring (consuming) fire (the witnesses noting the meaning — the visible glory of God, the shekinah, appearing as fire, the token of his holiness, majesty, and consuming justice, the lesson — God a consuming fire, the holiness that devours sin and the sinner, Deuteronomy 4:24, 'the LORD thy God is a consuming fire, even a jealous God'; 9:3; Hebrews 12:29, 'our God is a consuming fire,' the terror of the divine majesty, the glory dreadful to the people, the holiness before which sinful man cannot stand, the contrast, the devouring fire to the people below, the gracious vision to Moses within, vv.18, the same glory terrible to the unreconciled, gracious to the mediator and the reconciled, the awe of the holy God); the people seeing the glory as devouring fire (the dreadful appearance to those below) — the terror (the majesty that kept them at a distance, Exodus 19:12, 21); the devouring fire — the holiness and consuming justice of God (Deuteronomy 4:24; Hebrews 12:29, the providence, the glory terrible to the people, the law given amid the consuming fire, the contrast with the grace of the gospel, Hebrews 12:18-29) — Wesley, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the glory like devouring fire signify?

sides.position: The visible glory (the shekinah) appearing as consuming fire, the token of God's holiness, majesty, and justice (Deuteronomy 4:24; Hebrews 12:29) - the terror of the divine majesty before which sinful man cannot stand, dreadful to the people below yet gracious to the mediator within (vv.18)

themes: And the sight of the glory of the LORD was like devouring fire on the top of the mount in the eyes of the children of Israel
the glory of the LORD like devouring fire (the crux of the consuming majesty)
the token of God's holiness, majesty, and consuming justice (Deuteronomy 4:24; Hebrews 12:29)
dreadful to the people below, gracious to the mediator within (vv.18)

schools.summary: Moses entering the cloud and abiding forty days: 'and Moses went into the midst of the cloud, and gat him up into the mount: and Moses was in the mount forty days and forty nights' — Moses entering the very midst of the cloud (the glory, the consuming fire, vv.17), going up higher, and remaining on the mount forty days and forty nights (the entrance, the mediator going into the cloud of glory, where the people saw devouring fire, yet Moses graciously admitted, the forty days, the long communion, the receiving of the law and the pattern of the tabernacle, chs. 25-31); Moses entering the cloud (the glory, the consuming fire to the people, yet Moses admitted and preserved) — the gracious admission (the mediator in the presence, the type of Christ entering the holiest, Hebrews 9:24); 'forty days and forty nights' (the forty days, the long communion, Moses fasting, Deuteronomy 9:9, 'I abode in the mount forty days and forty nights, I neither did eat bread nor drink water,' the receiving of the law and the tabernacle pattern, chs. 25-31, the witnesses noting the parallel — the forty days of Moses, twice, Deuteronomy 9:18, of Elijah, 1 Kings 19:8, and of Christ, Matthew 4:2, the forty days' fast, the providence, the mediator long in the presence, receiving the revelation for the people) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Moses went into the midst of the cloud, and gat him up into the mount: and Moses was in the mount forty days and forty nights
Moses entering the cloud and abiding forty days
the mediator graciously admitted into the glory (a type of Christ, Hebrews 9:24)
the forty days' communion and fast (Deuteronomy 9:9; cf. Elijah, Christ, 1 Kings 19:8; Matthew 4:2)
